title: Understand: Pre-Civil War Debates Over Slavery
description: Check your understanding of the pre-Civil War debates over slavery with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction
Understand: Pre-Civil War Debates Over Slavery

assessment_items.question_content:
Abolition, the movement to end slavery, gained momentum in the early-to-mid 1800s. 

**What was true about the abolition movement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was made up of only free blacks and former enslaved person.
It included only wealthy whites from the New England states.
It was an interracial movement, bringing African American and white Americans, women and men alike, together in a common cause.
It only supported the expansion of slavery into western territories.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What did radical abolitionist William Lloyd Garrison believe about the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution was a pro-slavery document.
The Constitution was “a covenant with death and an agreement in hell.”
The Constitution was a “glorious liberty document.”
The Constitution was best understood as an anti-slavery document

assessment_items.question_content:
**Before the Civil War, which formerly enslaved person and influential abolitionist called the Constitution a “glorious liberty document” and urged the North to “now make that instrument bend to the cause of freedom and justice”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Frederick Douglass
Ella Baker
Martin Luther King, Jr.
Malcolm X

assessment_items.question_content:
Many anti-slavery advocates, including Abraham Lincoln, thought that the Constitution didn’t empower the national government to abolish slavery where it already existed. 

**These advocates \_\_\_\_.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: didn’t oppose slavery as long as slavery wasn’t practiced in their own state
believed the Constitution supported the idea of “freedom national, slavery local”
were not concerned about whether slavery expanded into western territories
believed that America would never abolish slavery, so they focused on improving conditions for enslaved people

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the key constitutional question in *Dred Scott v. Sandford*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Since Dred Scott was transported from a slave state to a free state, was he free?
Can the national government pass a law to end slavery in all states?
Is the Fugitive Slave Act constitutional?
Do the states have the right to end slavery by popular vote?

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the impact of the *Dred Scott* case?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: This was one of the key conclusions in the *Dred Scott* case, which denied citizenship to African Americans.
Congress had no power to determine whether to ban slavery in newly admitted states.
Free Black people could vote in national elections, but they were not able to vote in state or local elections.
The Missouri Compromise of 1820 was constitutional.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Who was the first anti-slavery president of the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Abraham Lincoln
Stephen A. Douglas
Andrew Jackson
James Madison

assessment_items.question_content:
Scholars refer to a group led by Salmon P. Chase as the “political abolitionists.” 

**Why are they called that?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They were willing to engage in electoral politics to end slavery.
They believed that the national government could abolish slavery in the South.
They worked to give formerly enslaved people the right to vote.
Their ultimate goal was to pass an amendment to overturn the Three-Fifths Clause.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the immediate result of the election of 1860?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Several slaveholding states in the South seceded from the Union.
The Republican government ratified several amendments ending slavery.
As his first act as president, Abraham Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation.
President Lincoln used his inaugural address to call on Congress to pass a national law abolishing slavery throughout the United States.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Phillis Wheatley contribute to the debate about slavery before the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: She argued that slavery was not compatible with the American Revolution’s principles of liberty and equality.
She advocated for the spread of slavery to new American territories throughout her writings.
She wrote newspaper articles about violence against Black people, highlighting the issue for the American public.
She was the author of several compromises on the issue of slavery, including the Missouri Compromise of 1820.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the Declaration of Independence used by the anti-slavery movement?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They used it to argue that the United States was rooted in human equality.
They used it to show that the Founding generation planned to end slavery.
They used it to prove that the federal government had the power to end slavery.
They did not use the Declaration of Independence, as many did not respect the ideas in the document.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the Missouri Compromise affect the practice of slavery in the United States?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It outlawed the spread of slavery outside of the original thirteen states.
It banned the international slave trade in the United States, leading to chattel slavery.
It gave all new states the power to choose whether to be a slave or free state.
It allowed the institution of slavery to spread to some new states but not all.
